items.q: What does a telescope's aperture primarily determine?
items.why: Students think bigger telescopes magnify more. Magnification depends on eyepiece. Aperture determines how much light you collect (faint objects visible) and resolution (fine detail visible). Big aperture = big telescope.

items.q: What is the 'habitable zone' around a star?
items.correct: The region where liquid water could exist on a planet's surface.
items.random_wrong: Where humans could survive without spacesuits.
items.why: Students assume habitable zone = inhabited or human-suitable. It's a NECESSARY but not sufficient condition for Earth-like life - based purely on temperature for liquid water; doesn't guarantee atmosphere or life.

items.q: What is the difference between an asteroid and a comet?
items.correct: Asteroids are rocky/metallic; comets contain ice that vaporises near the Sun, producing a tail.
items.misconception: They are the same object with different names.
items.why: Students treat both as 'space rocks'. The composition differs: comets have volatile ice that sublimes when warmed near the Sun (creating coma and tail); asteroids are rocky/metallic with no tail.

items.q: How many planets are in the Solar System (IAU 2006)?
items.why: It's 8. “12” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Who proposed the heliocentric model in 1543?
items.why: It's Copernicus. “Galileo” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Approximately how many million kilometres is 1 AU?
items.why: The real figure is 150 million km. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
